data.title: Opera: Great Financials, Growth Prospects, Low Margin Pressure
data.description:
Introduction

Opera (NASDAQ:OPRA) reported a stellar Q4'24 on February 28, significantly exceeding analyst's expectations and guiding for a great 2025. Shares rallied 10% on reporting the quarter, but somewhat surprisingly slid back down to -2% on the day. This article reviews Opera's business and history, considers their financials, proposes a valuation model according to which shares are undervalued by around 52%, briefly mentions risks, and then concludes.

Business

Opera is a browser company with a long history with a unique history that is worth commenting on. Founded in 1995, Norway-based Opera went public in 2018 after having been bought by a consortium of Chinese investors in 2016. The IPO priced the company at $12 per share, and today the ADR trades at around $18.

The company was operationally break-even until the year 2022. For a period, Opera pivoted to providing loans in developing countries - apparently completely out of line with its core business. During this period, Hindenburg Research even wrote a critical report of the company, noting that the loan business in 2020 accounted for 42% of revenues and highlighting what they called questionable related-party transactions. In March 2022, Opera divested its stake in Nanobank, the fintech business used to provide loans in Africa and Asia which Opera acquired a stake in 2020, and returned to focus on its core business. This coincided with a strategic shift away from developing regions toward higher revenue generating users in North America and Europe. While Monthly Active Users ("MAUs") declined from 324 million in 2022 to 296 million in Q4'24, Annualized Average Revenue Per User ("ARPU") has increased significantly from a meager $0.41 in Q1 of 2020 to $1.97 in Q4'24.

This strategic shift has been a major success, largely attributable to two moves: the gaming browser Opera GX and integrating AI search functionality into its browser. Opera GX is monetizing incredibly well at an annualized ARPU of $3.68 in Q4'24 and the browser reached 33.9 million MAUs, an 22% increase YoY. Being early with integrating AI features with the built-in Aria assistant launched almost two years ago has also helped increase user retention and offer a better overall experience.

Financials

Opera's revenues come from two main segments: Advertising (around 65% of revenue) through its own ad platform and E-commerce partnerships and Search (around 35% of revenues) which takes a cut out of the ad revenue generated by its Google integrated search engine. Opera has a healthy $126.8 million in cash and zero debt. They have consistently beaten guidance and Q4'24 was no different. Judging from Google Trends data so far into Q1'25, it looks like Opera has increased or at the very least sustained interest in its browser products. In Q3 2024, the Google Trends quarterly mean was 46.3 and so far, in Q1 2025, it is 55.25.

There is little to complain about in this income statement. Revenues have grown healthily, gross profit margin has improved, operating margin has improved, and Google Trends suggest a continued interest in Opera's products.

Valuation

Opera reported $480.6 million in revenue for 2024 - a 171% increase from when it posted revenues of $177 million in 2020. I think my case below is more reasonable than what looks to be priced in. Even if we don't see 15% growth, Opera's model should be able to run light operationally to increase FCF margins. The models below assume analyst estimates until 2026 and growth assumption after.

Opera has no pure publicly traded peers, but the company does not look comparatively expensive on a multiples basis.

Risks

While the financials are looking great, growth prospects reasonable with little margin pressure, and recent actions by management and the board have favored share and ADR holders (such as paying out dividends semi-annually), there are some risks, primarily relating to having a Chinese consortium as a majority owner. Chinese stocks in general have been trading at a discount in recent times, although sentiments appear to be improving as the Chinese Communist Party has been signaling a more pro business attitude. Xi Jinping has also tried to appease western companies, likely to counter a decline in foreign direct investment, which has plummeted in recent years. Even in the light of this, some like Carson Block argue that China remains uninvestable. The other issue relating to a Chinese majority owner is the amount of data Opera generates on its users. This could pose both a privacy related backlash amongst users, but also regulatory/political issues, as we saw with TikTok. Still, it is rarely mentioned that having this majority owner is also a benefit, as they are involved in multiple other software and IT companies whose R&D could also benefit Opera.

Conclusion

Opera is certainly an interesting company that has carved out a niche in the browser market. Management's decisions in recent years have yielded fruit, and shareholders have been treated favorably despite its majority ownership and numerous related-party risks noted in their 10K's risk factors segment. I think this has around 50% upside from these levels.

data.title: Yellow Cake: Re-Evaluating The Uranium Trade After The Sell-Off
data.description:
Introduction

With uranium and uranium miners having fallen significantly over the last 12 months, I am getting interested again in the uranium trade.

Uranium spot prices, after peaking at almost $110 per pound at the beginning of 2024, have retreated by almost 40%.

This happened despite no relevant change in the fundamentals of the uranium market during the last year, leaving uranium investors disappointed and disgruntled. I believe there is now an opportunity to re-enter the market. The uranium thesis remains alive, but expectations must be tempered by reality.

Reflections on the recent price dynamics

The uranium spot price had gotten ahead of itself in January 2024. Uranium spot prices usually trade at a discount to long-term prices. The difference between spot and long-term prices is that spot prices are for immediate delivery, represent only 10-20% of global uranium trades, and are heavily influenced by speculators; long-term prices are for future delivery (typically three to 10 years out), constitute the majority of the market, and are based on contracts between producers and utilities. The spot price can spike above the long-term price, but it always mean reverts. In January 2024, the spot premium was at one of its highest levels in history, signaling excessive speculative exuberance.

The information signal embedded in uranium spot prices can be quite low. There are multiple reasons for that.

First of all, the uranium spot market is thinly traded, and hence highly volatile. The uranium spot market is remarkably tiny compared with most other commodities, despite its absolute centrality to the energy security of many countries. Uranium spot transactions occur sporadically, sometimes weeks apart. A single large purchase or sale can distort prices significantly. For example, a single utility having to cover up a shortfall by buying half a million pounds of uranium in the spot market can cause a temporary spike. Similarly, small producers dumping a few hundred thousand pounds for liquidity reasons can have the opposite effect on spot prices.

The uranium spot market is also highly opaque. Uranium is not traded on a public exchange. Prices are negotiated privately between buyers and sellers, with limited disclosure of terms. Price reporting agencies (like UxC and TradeTech) rely on voluntary reporting by market participants, which can lead to incomplete or biased data.

The structure of the uranium spot market has changed dramatically in recent years with the emergence of new speculative investors. This has led to an increase in volatility, both to the upside and to the downside. In particular, by hoarding physical uranium, the Sprott Physical Uranium Trust (SPUT) can drive short-term price swings to the upside. When the Trust trades at a premium to NAV, it issues new shares, whose proceedings, by the Trust's mandate, are immediately re-invested to buy even more uranium. This mechanism drove the first wave of uranium repricing in the 2021 to 2023 period, but was not responsible for the most recent spike in the second half of 2023 and ending at the beginning of 2024, as the following plot confirms.

Even if the Sprott Uranium Trust was not a significant player in the 2023 price spike, speculative activity was still an important factor, driven by geopolitical concerns about security of supply following the Russian invasion of Ukraine. Another important factor was a sharp increase in the uranium conversion and enrichment prices, which caused a scramble for uranium feedstock and further tightened the uranium market. Let us remind the reader that, when prices for enriched uranium are high, enrichers consume more uranium than normal. This phenomenon, known as overfeeding, essentially represents an extra variable source of uranium demand.

In 2024, spot prices mean reverted towards long-term prices. Market sentiment hence shifted in the opposite direction. Investors' focus concentrated on the mine restarts, as both Kazatomprom and Cameco have ramped up production to take advantage of strong uranium prices. The election of Donald Trump has also given rise to hopes that a normalization of relations with Russia is possible, thus reducing a possible geopolitical premium. Finally, enthusiasm about AI and small modular reactors seems to have ebbed recently, undermining investors' confidence in future nuclear energy demand.

I believe that the essential point to keep in mind is that, while uranium spot prices have fallen by 40%, uranium long-term prices, as well as conversion and enrichment prices, have not fallen and, in fact, are still rising. But uranium spot prices are an unreliable indicator. Long-term prices, on the other hand, reflect the long-term fundamentals and are trending upwards.

To summarize, all market indicators are suggesting a tightening supply/demand balance, except one. But the exceptional indicator is also known to be the most volatile and noisy. My natural inclination is to disregard the latter indicator, and trust in the others.

The uranium supply/demand picture

I believe that many retail uranium investors have unrealistic expectations, combined with a lack of patience. Both stem from a fundamental misconception about the nature of the uranium market.

Uranium is remarkably different from other energy commodities like oil. In the case of oil, it is much easier to predict its supply and demand picture a few quarters away, rather than a decade away. The opposite is true for uranium. As a consequence, price discovery for uranium proceeds very slowly. At the same time, there is considerably less uncertainty about its direction.

Uranium primary supply is relatively slow to react to price increases. New uranium mines can take years, if not decades, to go from exploration to production, and are also some of the most difficult assets in the world to permit. Production from existing operations cannot be ramped up quickly. Both Cameco and Kazatomprom have encountered significant challenges and disappointed guidance in recent quarters.

Secondary sources of supply, however, can enter the market rapidly. They are hard to quantify due to the extreme opacity of reporting. Secondary supply represents the greatest threat to the uranium thesis. My belief is that secondary sources are drying up, and therefore will be unable to alter the long-term supply/demand picture. My reason to think so is that we have already seen secondary supply significantly decline in recent years. Secondary sources of supply have decreased even from 2021 onwards, despite higher uranium prices. If there were vast secondary sources of uranium, it seems reasonable that we should have seen them come back into the market by now.

The demand picture for uranium is quite bullish. Uranium demand is inelastic because it takes years to build a new nuclear reactor, and its uranium fuel requirements are fixed and predictable. It is therefore possible to project future uranium demand quite accurately. The cost of nuclear fuel is insignificant compared with the capex cost to build a new nuclear reactor. Hence, nuclear plant operators are relatively price insensitive: even if uranium prices were to spike, they would be forced to buy at any price to keep the lights on. The following plot shows that, while EU utilities have a coverage of about 50% through the early 2030s, US utilities are far more exposed from 2029 onwards. New contracting has been below expectations in 2024. But, as the pace of new contracts picks up, price discovery should accelerate, and uranium long-term prices move higher.

There are currently 440 operable reactors in the world. 65 are under construction, 86 are already planned. A further 344 reactors have been proposed, according to the World Nuclear Association. Small modular reactors (SMRs) also have the potential to contribute significantly to future uranium demand. I believe, however, this is a long-duration driver that will not matter over the next few years. Thus, uranium demand is set to increase by at least a third over the next decade, and potentially double, or more.

In conclusion, the world is going to need more uranium going forward. The green energy bubble is bursting, and an increasing number of countries are realizing that nuclear energy is the only reliable, low-emission source of baseload power. While demand is set to increase, supply is highly constrained. It is hard to imagine a scenario where primary supply surprises to the upside, without uranium prices significantly rising from current levels. The reason is that, with uranium currently at $65 per pound, part of the global production cost curve remains still above it. Higher prices are therefore needed to incentivize new production.

How to invest

Given the recent fall-off in uranium spot prices from overextended territory, and the unchanged long-term fundamentals, I see the current price as a good entry point for new investors.

There are two main investment vehicles available to speculate on uranium prices: the Sprott Physical Uranium Trust (OTCPK:SRUUF) and Yellow Cake (OTCQX:YLLXF). At the moment, my choice falls on Yellow Cake, for two different reasons.

First of all, SPUT has lower costs, but this comes with a disadvantage. When uranium prices spike, investors' demand is likely to increase, and SPUT to trade at a premium to NAV. When SPUT trades at a premium, it has to buy new uranium. In short, SPUT buys high and never sells. On the other hand, Yellow Cake does not have such a strict mandate. Yellow Cake can discretionally do an equity raising, and use the proceedings to buy more uranium. The company has the option through 2027 to buy up to $100 million per year in uranium oxide (yellowcake) from Kazatomprom. This gives flexibility: given the volatility of spot prices, Yellow Cake can take advantage of market conditions. It can time its purchases when the spot price is lower. Potentially, it could also sell when prices are particularly high, and pay out the proceedings via a special dividend, thus providing an exit strategy for shareholders.

The second reason is that Yellow Cake trades at a far larger discount to NAV than SPUT. As of today (February 28), SPUT trades at a 9.6% discount, while Yellow Cake at a 16.2% discount. Even if Yellow Cake has slightly higher costs, these are generally negligible (<1% of NAV). Except for the fact that Yellow Cake trades on the OTC market and its main listing is on the London Stock Exchange (ticker: YCA), I see no fundamental reason that justifies the higher discount placed on Yellow Cake.

Conclusion

The recent 40% decline in uranium spot prices presents a compelling entry point. Speculative froth and transient factors can distort spot prices in the short term, but rising long-term contract prices across all flue cycle market segments signal that the fundamentals have not changed. Demand for uranium is set to increase by more than a third over the next decade. Meanwhile, supply is likely to struggle to keep pace amid protracted mine development cycles and geopolitical constraints. For investors willing to exercise patience, Yellow Cake stands out as a strategic vehicle, offering exposure at a steep discount to NAV.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hims & Hers: A Generational Dip-Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction

Hims & Hers (NYSE:HIMS) — the hypergrowth telehealth company on a mission to make the world feel great through the power of better health — is going bananas.

Since its bottom in May 2022, the stock has run more than 2,000% from $3 to $70 a share. Extreme undervaluation, exploding fundamentals, and the GLP-1 hype serve as mega tailwinds for the stock.

I have written Strong-Buy article after Strong-Buy article on HIMS since 2021, but despite its meteoric rise, I believe HIMS stock is still undervalued with significant upside potential over the next decade.

While it is a volatile stock with frequent 10% to 20% moves in either direction, the company's stellar execution, improving fundamentals, and relative undervaluation have made it particularly easy to hold the stock through all this volatility.

Speaking of which, HIMS stock recently sold off by about 50% in just four trading days as a barrage of "negative" news drove an onslaught of panic selling.

To many, this seems like the beginning of the end of an incredible rally.

To me, it's nothing but a generational buy-the-dip opportunity.

Growth: Still a Baby

Looking at the company's Q4 results, HIMS ended 2024 with 2.2M Subscribers, up 45% YoY. On a sequential basis, HIMS added 182K Net New Subscribers, which is the second highest in a single quarter.

Management attributed the growth of Subscribers to the expansion of personalized treatments, which include unique form factors, multi-condition treatments, and personalized dosages.

The number of Subscribers is one of, if not, the most important metric to track for high-growth companies like HIMS, as user growth is a leading indicator of future Revenue growth, so to see the company quadruple its Subscriber base over the last three years from 554K at the end of 2021, reflects significant Revenue potential for the company.

More importantly, the addition of each new Subscriber will strengthen the platform via increased brand trust, data insights, product innovation, and clinical experience, creating a virtuous cycle that leads to ever-improving outcomes for Subscribers on the platform.

As a result of rapid Subscriber growth, Revenue grew to a record $481M in Q4, up a staggering 95% YoY, with growth accelerating 18pp QoQ. This beat both the high end of management's guidance and average analyst estimates by $11M.

Of course, much of this Revenue growth was due to the recent launch of its weight loss offerings, which include oral weight loss and compounded GLP-1 injections — the latter had a more prominent impact on growth. For context, 2024 Revenue, including GLP-1 offerings, was $1.5B, up 69% YoY. Excluding GLP-1 offerings, Revenue would have been $1.2B, up 43% YoY. This is still an impressive growth rate, nonetheless, and it also signifies the "platform's growing ability to enter new specialties and scale rapidly", which positions the company well "to serve tens of millions of individuals over time".

Our GLP-1 offering, which launched in the second quarter of 2024, has experienced similar success, delivering north of $225 million of incremental revenue in 2024.

(CFO Yemi Okupe — HIMS FY2024 Q4 Earnings Call)

The launch of oral weight loss (starting at $69/month) in Q4 2023 and GLP-1 injections (starting at $165/month) in Q2 2024 also contributed to higher Revenue per Subscriber, which was $73/month as of Q4, up 38% YoY. The shift to more premium personalized offerings also drove this growth.

That being said, we can expect Revenue per Subscriber to increase further over the next few quarters as more Subscribers adopt more personalized treatments, including multi-condition treatments and oral weight loss, as well as liraglutide (an FDA-approved generic GLP-1 injection), which is projected to roll out later this year.

Now with liraglutide, it's a bit of a challenge because you have more medication that’s needed, given it's a daily injection versus a weekly injection. You've got more sterile needles that are required on a weekly basis given this. So everything from raw API to packaging is more expensive. So I would suspect that there's going to be some trickle-down effect that results in us having a similar price point to the commercial dosing in the couple of hundred bucks range.

(CEO Andrew Dudum — HIMS FY2024 Q4 Earnings Call)

Given all the drama about branded GLP-1 drugs, semaglutide shortage, and compounding regulation, let's get to the most important part of the report: guidance.

Considering HIMS' strong exit from 2024, management expects the company's growth momentum to continue in 2025:

  • Q1 2025 Revenue is expected to be $520M to $540M, implying an 87% to 94% YoY growth. This beats analyst estimates of $495M.
  • FY2025 Revenue is expected to be $2.3B to $2.4B, implying a 56% to 63% YoY growth. This beats analyst estimates of $2.1B.

Embedded in their guidance, management anticipates 2025 Weight Loss Revenue of at least $725M. Per management:

This figure excludes contributions from commercially available dosages of semaglutide, which will not be offered on the platform after the first quarter. There may be the potential to offer commercially available dosages of compounded semaglutide throughout the year.

However, we see our steady state weight loss offering being primarily composed of our evolving oral based solutions as well as liraglutide later this year. Personalized semaglutide dosages will supplement these core offerings for the subset of consumers for whom it is a clinical necessity.

(CFO Yemi Okupe — HIMS FY2024 Q4 Earnings Call)

In the guidance slide of the company's investor presentation, management included the following footnote:

On February 21, 2025, the FDA resolved the semaglutide shortage, which could constrain our ability to continue providing access to compounded semaglutide on our platform once our current inventory has been sold.

(HIMS FY2024 Q4 Investor Presentation Slide 38)

In addition, according to the FDA, "to avoid unnecessary disruption to patient treatment and to help facilitate an orderly transition", the FDA is providing a grace period of 60 days for 503a pharmacies until April 22 and 90 days for 503b pharmacies until May 22. After these dates, the FDA will not allow the "compounding, distributing, or dispensing" of "semaglutide injection products that are essentially a copy of a commercially available drug product".

Okay, I want you to read the last few paragraphs on guidance a few more times because it is quite confusing at first.

Done? Okay, let me expand further.

In other words, if we connect the dots, that "at least $725M" of Weight Loss Revenue:

  • ... does not include "commercially available dosages of compounded semaglutide" even though HIMS can sell these drugs until April or May, or until their "current inventory has been sold", or potentially, "throughout the year".
  • ... includes mainly its oral weight loss solutions and liraglutide, with limited sales from its "personalized" semaglutide offering. The keyword here is "personalized" because the personalization element of HIMS' semaglutide offering — whether through customized titration schedules and/or dosage levels — should eliminate HIMS from being "essentially a copy of a commercially available drug product", thus allowing HIMS to continue selling its personalized compounded GLP-1 offering. Whatever it is, oral-based solutions and liraglutide are the "core" of the guidance, while personalized semaglutide dosages are the "supplement".

In short, management's $725M+ Weight Loss Revenue guidance has minimal contributions from its personalized dosages of semaglutide and no commercially available dosages of semaglutide (which is why they blacked the segment out in the slide below).

Put differently, since they are not including the sales of commercially available dosages of semaglutide in their guidance, I believe management's guidance is extremely understated — perhaps hundreds of millions of dollars lower than actual expectations.

That being said, I'm not too worried about the short-term volatility caused by the GLP-1 drama because the HIMS investment thesis never revolved around GLP-1 drugs in the first place — it's just nice to have.

Besides, its core offerings outside of GLP-1s are still growing rapidly at a 43% growth rate in 2024. More importantly, HIMS continues to gain market share, now making up 47%+ of the highly competitive telehealth space. This is a testament to the company's strong execution, brand strength, and technological prowess.

This is why management is doubling down on the company, investing heavily in its platform, its breadth of offerings, its facility capacity, its personalization capabilities, its AI technology (MedMatch), its marketing, and even its workforce (HIMS is within weeks of announcing a new CTO).

Ultimately, all these investments will enable HIMS to fulfill its next major milestone of reaching 10M Subscribers, which CEO Andrew Dudum hopes to achieve within the next five to six years.

As long as the HIMS team remains hyperfocused on providing consumers access to "high-quality, precision care that is convenient and affordable", I have no doubt that HIMS will achieve its vision of being the next-generation healthcare platform, eventually amassing more than 100M Subscribers across all corners of the healthcare market.

With only 2.2M Subscribers as of Q4, I can say with utmost confidence that HIMS' growth story is still in its baby phase.

Profitability: Super Bowl Ad Was A Huge Win

Turning to profitability, Gross Profit for the quarter was $370M, up 81% YoY. Gross Margin dropped 6pp YoY and 2pp QoQ to 77%. This was mainly due to the higher costs of the new weight loss category, which was launched in Q4 of 2023. As you can see, Gross Margin started to decline in Q4 of 2023.

In addition, lower prices on some of its subscription plans have also pressured Gross Margin. For instance, HIMS recently dropped its oral weight loss solutions and GLP-1 injections from $79/month and $199/month, respectively, to $69/month and $165/month, respectively.

With this recent price cut, HIMS now offers the lowest annual cost of any GLP-1 weight loss subscription plan on the market. Even then, over the long-term, management intends to reduce prices further — to as low as $100/month — which should subsequently attract even more consumers to the platform.

That said, expect Gross Margin to continue its decline in Q1 before recovering in Q2 as HIMS gains economies of scale.

We expect margins to start to recover in the second quarter as we reach milestones for pre-adjusted volume driven discounts across our operation, with further opportunity as we expand sterile fulfillment capabilities within our affiliated facilities later this year.

(CFO Yemi Okupe — HIMS FY2024 Q4 Earnings Call)

Scale combined with operational excellence has enabled HIMS to pass on the savings to consumers. Despite declining Gross Margins, Adjusted EBITDA Margin expanded 3pp YoY and 1pp QoQ to 11%. On an absolute basis, Q4 Adjusted EBITDA was a record $54M, improving 163% YoY. This was in line with management's guidance and analyst expectations.

Net Income was $26M in Q4, up from just $1M last year. GAAP EPS was $0.11, in line with analyst estimates.

Adjusted EBITDA Margin improvement was driven by operating leverage across all expense lines. Most notably, HIMS continues to gain marketing leverage with Marketing Expenses as a % of Revenue dropping 5pp YoY to 46%. Moving forward, management expects to "drive between 1-3 points of marketing leverage per annum over the course of the next few years".

Many bears argue that HIMS relies too heavily on Marketing Expenses to drive growth. Well, at this stage of its growth cycle, I believe HIMS should maintain its aggressive marketing spend to maximize Subscriber growth and product adoption.

Besides, HIMS' unique, but somewhat controversial, marketing strategy has proven to be incredibly effective — just look at their growth over the last few years.

An example of its marketing success would be the recent Superbowl ad campaign, which reverberated through the entire healthcare industry. According to EDO, HIMS' Super Bowl ad drove 7.7x as much engagement as the average Super Bowl ad airing and was ranked the #5 most effective ad among the 94 brand ads aired this year.

While HIMS' 1-minute Super Bowl ad cost the company about $15M (a 30-second slot costs about $7M to $8M), the value it gets in return should be many times more. In any case, given its strong unit economics with a payback period of approximately half a year, HIMS will easily earn back that $15M. Judging by the EDO stats, this Super Bowl ad was money well spent, and this ad alone should lead to monumental Subscriber gains in Q1.

That being said, as a result of its strategic pricing actions and marketing investments, expect some pressure on Adjusted EBITDA Margin in Q1, but just like Gross Margin, expect it to improve in Q2 and beyond. Here's what management guided:

  • Q1 Adjusted EBITDA of $55M to $65M, implying an 11% to 12% Margin. This beat analyst estimates of $59M.
  • FY2025 Adjusted EBITDA of $270M to $320M, implying a 12% to 13% Margin. This beat analyst estimates of $275M.

My view is that management is lowballing their guidance. One, they have a history of sandbagging and outperforming their guidance. Two, it is my belief that this Adjusted EBITDA guidance does not include Revenue from "commercially available dosages of compounded semaglutide".

Whatever it is, the most important thing is that HIMS continues to drive operating leverage while reinvesting in the business for future growth. Not short-term, artificial growth, but long-term, durable growth.

From what I'm seeing, HIMS is delivering more and more value to its Subscribers — whether in the form of higher-quality products, better technological experience, or more affordable prices — and this should pay massive dividends in the future.

As HIMS continues to scale, I expect Adjusted EBITDA Margins to march higher, ultimately reaching management's long-term target of 20% to 30%.

Rapid growth and improving profitability is a recipe for earnings explosion — I expect the stock to follow suit as earnings trend higher over time.

Health: Double Acquisition Supercharges Category Expansion

As of Q4, HIMS has Cash and Investments of $300M with zero debt on its balance sheet. As you can see, its cash position has been growing over the past few quarters.

Balance sheet growth was driven by a record Operating Cash Flow of $86M, which is up 293% YoY. OCF Margin was 18% in Q4, improving 9pp YoY.

Free Cash Flow was $60M in Q4, growing 452% YoY. FCF Margin was 12%, which increased 8pp YoY. CapEx was a record $27M in Q4, which includes the $15M acquisition of Medisource, the company's first 503b compounding outsourcing facility.

With robust cash flows and strong cash levels, management upped their acquisition game, acquiring not one but two more companies in Q1:

  • Trybe Labs: This is a provider of at-home laboratory kits, allowing HIMS to introduce affordable at-home testing for Subscribers. At-home blood draws and other whole-body testing will not only unlock even more data and health insights for HIMS but also enable the company to provide better, more precise personalized treatments for Subscribers. In addition, it will accelerate the company's expansion to other intensive categories like low testosterone and menopausal support.
  • US-based Peptide Facility: This acquisition strengthens the company's domestic supply chain. Peptide capabilities will also allow HIMS to explore peptide innovation and consider expanding to new specialties such as pain management, longevity, and muscle recovery.

Alongside continued investments in its 503a and 503b facilities, these recent acquisitions further verticalize the HIMS platform, increase the breadth of personalized treatments, and set the foundation for expansion into new categories.

In addition, HIMS will gain access to even more data, accelerating the company's AI developments (including MedMatch) as it creates an AI-powered healthcare experience that leads to more effective solutions and better health outcomes.

All in all, these investments solidify HIMS' growth story as it built itself into the healthcare platform of the future.

Valuation: Up 79% YTD, Still Cheap

HIMS stock has gone through a roller coaster ride recently. The stock rallied hard in January, propelled by strong market sentiment and Trump's inauguration.

However, the bulk of its move came in February when the stock broke past its previous highs of $35 a share, triggering a short squeeze — did I mention that HIMS had a short ratio of 25%+? Then, hype about the company's Super Bowl ad and its potential impact began to surface. In addition, the company announced its acquisition of Trybe Labs, bringing even more excitement.

As a result, the stock surged from $35 to $70 in a matter of two short weeks.

However, the stock collapsed following semaglutide's removal from the shortage list — which was bound to happen anyway — cutting the stock price by half.

Now, the stock is trading at $45 a share, up 79% YTD.

As it stands, HIMS stock is trading at a Price to TTM Sales ratio of 7.2x. Based on analysts' 2025 Revenue estimate of $2.3B, that multiple drops to just 4.3x. In my opinion, HIMS will likely beat the high end of its Revenue guidance of $2.4B, which means that its forward multiple should be lower, thus making the stock cheaper.

That being said, a TTM multiple of 7.2x and a forward multiple of 4.3x — while not as cheap as they used to be — are still inexpensive, given HIMS' strong execution, growing moats, and improving fundamentals.

Moreover, institutions are loading up on HIMS stock, potentially offering some level of downside protection. As HIMS scales further and becomes more profitable with each passing quarter, there's also rising speculation that HIMS could be added to the S&P MidCap 400 Index, which could serve as a major catalyst for the stock.

In my last article, I realized I had severely underestimated HIMS' growth potential, especially considering its recent acquisitions and category expansion plans. As such, I am increasing my Revenue projections for HIMS.

On the other hand, I might have overestimated HIMS' long-term margin profile, which is why I'm revising down HIMS' long-term FCF Margin.

That being said, here are my updated DCF assumptions on HIMS stock:

  • FY2034 Revenue of $12B, assuming 62% growth in 2025 (in line with guidance) and 30% growth in 2026. Over time, I expect growth to slow down to just 15% by 2034.
  • FY2034 FCF Margin of 20%, translating to an Adjusted EBITDA Margin of 25%, which is the midpoint of management's long-term target of 20% to 30%.
  • Perpetual Growth Rate of 3.0%.
  • Discount Rate of 12%.

Based on these assumptions, I arrive at a 12-month price target of $70 a share for HIMS stock. This was raised from $45 previously — coincidentally, the stock is currently trading at $45 as of this writing. Regardless, $70 represents an upside potential of 56%.

My bear case target is $48, while my bull case target is $101.

In short, despite being up 79% YTD, I believe HIMS stock is still significantly undervalued.

Risks

  • Acquisition Spree: HIMS has acquired three companies in the last six months, which is a lot given the company's size. My concern is that these acquisitions may not create the desired synergies that management is looking for, resulting in unnecessary costs, subpar product quality, and added structural complexities. While I'm confident in management's ability to execute, I would also not be surprised if problems arise from these acquisitions.
  • Possible "Personalization" Ban: As I have thoroughly discussed earlier, management's Revenue guidance does not include "commercially available dosages of compounded semaglutide" but it does include "personalized dosages of semaglutide". In essence, personalized dosages bypass commercially available dosages by modifying dosage levels, such that they will not be deemed as essential copies of the commercial products. This is undeniably a grey area in the pharma industry, which could go in either direction: business as usual or a possible ban. While the HIMS investment thesis never hinges on GLP-1 drugs in the first place, a ban on selling personalized GLP-1s would surely hurt HIMS stock. And while management is confident that they'll be able to continue to offer personalized dosages of semaglutide, I wouldn't write off the possibility of an outright ban.

All of this is very clear and regulated. And so we play by the rules and the rules are quite clear. And so I don't expect there to be meaningful conflict when it comes to the personalization standpoint. I think historically, the broad industry, inclusive of the pharmaceutical companies have recognized and respected, I think, the need for this type of clinical personalization of medication.

(CEO Andrew Dudum — HIMS FY2024 Q4 Earnings Call)

Thesis

HIMS stock has been wild — it's a battleground stock caught in a GLP-1 drama and a controversial telehealth business model.

Despite the backlash from big pharma and bears, HIMS continues to expand its ecosystem as more and more consumers look for high-quality medical care at an affordable price point. In addition, its refreshing mobile app and tech platform make healthcare a much more approachable and delightful experience.

Bears argue that the HIMS growth story is busted, given that semaglutide is now taken off the shortage list. In addition, there's the potential for HIMS to face an outright ban from selling personalized, compounded GLP-1 solutions.

My question is: Have you forgotten that HIMS business outside of GLP-1s is growing 43% in 2024?

Additionally, now that the semaglutide shortage is over, investors can now put the GLP-1 drama behind them and instead focus on its core business, which is still in hyper growth mode.

Having said that, I remain very bullish on HIMS stock — even with its market-crushing YTD performance, HIMS stock is still trading at undervalued territories, presenting significant upside potential for investors.

With only 2.2M Subscribers as of Q4, HIMS has so much growth potential ahead as it continues to disrupt the traditional healthcare industry.

And considering its recent pullback, I believe this is a generational dip-buying opportunity for investors.

While 2024 was an exciting year, we believe that we have just begun to scratch the surface of the potential of our platform’s value to society.

(CEO Andrew Dudum — H&H FY2024 Letter to Shareholders)

data.title: Canadian Natural Resources: Tariff Risk Largely Mitigated By Crashing Canadian Dollar
data.description:
Markets sold off last week after Trump confirmed his intention to move forward with 25% tariffs on Mexico and Canada, which will go into effect on March 4th. Of course, as we saw last month, we cannot know whether this will truly go into effect until then. However, his administration has made it clear that it does not feel the two countries are doing enough to secure the border.

In my view, the most important outcome of these tariffs will be their impact on the energy market. Trump intends to place a lower 10% tax on Canadian energy imports, which will not have the same impact as a 25% tariff but is still likely to upset the energy market. This tariff is expected to raise prices for US refineries and potentially lower refinery output in midwestern states. Canada accounts for just over half of all US oil imports.

The key issue is the regional impact, as the US does not have sufficient oil pipelines and transportation capacity to move oil from producing areas to midwestern refineries. Unlike coastal refineries, these refineries depend on Canadian oil pipelines and cannot switch sources. Further, around 97% of Canadian oil exports go to the US, so Canada cannot easily switch its export location without a massive infrastructure overhaul.

In theory, tariffs should encourage Canadian exporters and US midstream companies to divert toward the Midwest. However, there is no immediate free market for oil because of immense physical infrastructure and engineering barriers to shifting buyers and sellers. Creating pipelines to untangle the US-Canadian oil trade will take many years and significant capital investment. Thus, it is unclear who the end payee of the Canadian oil tariff will be. Refineries in the Midwest may opt to produce less due to higher costs, likely pushing gasoline prices up in those states to meet the added cost. Thus, Canadian oil producers may see slightly lower sales, while many US consumers may see higher gasoline prices.

We still do not know if these tariffs will go into effect. Given their objective economic inefficiency, at least until new pipelines and transportation investments can be made, it is also unclear how long these tariffs will last. Still, certain Canadian companies, such as Canadian Natural Resources (NYSE:CNQ), are likely to be impacted. CNQ is currently down around 9% this year, while the US Energy Sector ETF (XLE) is up about 7%. This implies the market is reacting to the news by expecting lower profits for Canadian Natural Resources and higher profits for US energy companies. US energy companies generally should benefit from higher product prices via domestic production protection.

I last had a bullish outlook on CNQ last February because, at that time, it seemed North American producers had a distinct edge in light of the geopolitical conflict in the Middle East. I shifted toward a neutral view in the June article "Canadian Natural Resources: Rising Premium To Peer Group Points To Lower Relative Performance," warning that the company's valuation premium was excessive. CNQ has since fallen by 20% as its valuation premium has closed, oil prices have failed to rise, and it faces added risk due to tariffs. Given this, I believe it is an excellent time to reconsider CNQ's profit and valuation potential.

CNQ's Tariff Risk is Currency Dependent

In general, because the US produces oil domestically and imports from many countries, Canadian producers will likely see lower sales to the US if 10% tariffs go into effect. Although a 10% oil and gas tariff is not massive, buyers will always look for the lowest price, which will not be on Canadian oil. In January, Alberta's Premier warned that Canada must prepare for energy tariffs, relating that her conversation with Trump implied the tariffs would go into effect.

Canadian Natural Resources was already in a slightly challenged position. In October, the company posted strong Q3 earnings but said it would reduce natural gas grilling due to low prices, lowering its overall well-completion outlook. That reflects the general trend in the US, with American energy companies maintaining a lower rig count due to oil being at the breakeven price for newly drilled wells. The Canadian rig count is more volatile but has been stronger, potentially due to exceptionally high US demand for Canadian oil. See below:

Canadian Natural Resources is a major benefactor of the low CAD-USD exchange rate. To a large degree, the decline in the Canadian dollar may offset the tariff risk, mainly if the Canadian dollar sells off more in response to lower demand from tariffs. US demand for Canadian goods will decline due to 25% tariffs, likely pushing the currency's trade value lower. However, I do not expect demand for Canadian oil and natural gas to fall by the same degree, due to lower tariffs and the fact the US is mainly dependent on these imports. Thus, there is some potential that CNQ's sales will not be harmed as the CAD may fall by an additional 10%.

As you can see below, CNQ's profit increase since 2020 is partially driven by a lower CAD-USD:

CNQ is doing business in Canadian dollars, and declines in the Canadian dollar have not yet resulted in an inflation spike that would disproportionately raise its operating costs. Canada's YoY inflation rate is low at 1.9%, while its unemployment rate has risen to 6.7%, potentially signaling a high recession risk. Theoretically, a recession may benefit CNQ by lowering the CAD-USD exchange rate and lowering wage and capital cost inflation, which has been high in recent years.

Overall, I think investors and analysts should account for CNQ's tariff risk, but understand that exchange rate impacts heavily offset it. We generally see a stronger US dollar in response to tariffs, as they should lower US demand for other currencies. This is particularly true for highly impacted currencies like CAD-USD, which have already fallen over the past year to benefit exporters like CNQ. Although the US dollar may eventually reverse if tariffs harm the domestic economy or result in hefty reciprocal tariffs, I generally expect the energy tariff burden to fall on Canadian importers, who will have lower trade power. CNQ's sales to the US may slip, but I expect the result to be only around 1-3%, given the anticipated impact on exchange rates.

What is Canadian Natural Resources Worth?

The company's Q4 results will be released on March 6th. Analysts expect an EPS of $0.67, down 22% year over year. The consensus expects EPS to be around $2.5-$2.7 over the coming years before potentially rebounding in 2026. Given the lack of change in oil prices, these assumptions are reasonable. Tariffs may cause negative changes, but that is exchange rate dependent.

The rise of US energy stocks over recent months while Canadian counterparts decline has largely closed CNQ's valuation premium. CNQ's forward "P/E" of 11.1X is just below the sector median at 11.5X. Its forward "EV/EBITDA" of 5.6X is significantly below the sector median of 6.6X. CNQ's dividend yield of 5.5% is also well above the sector median of 4.2%.

The company's production is very balanced between natural gas (27%), heavy oil (26%), light oil (11%), and synthetic crude (36%). Its natural gas segment was an area of weakness in 2024, but its prospects have improved, given Henry Hub is now back to about $3.9/MMBTU due to cooler winter weather and stagnant North American output. Across the industry, capital investments into drilling have been low since ~2023, potentially creating a bullish long-term opportunity for energy prices.

Overall, I think CNQ is fairly valued today and potentially undervalued. Canadian Natural Resources has a lower valuation than most energy firms, with a particularly high dividend and lower debt leverage. Its debt-to-EBITDA ratio is only around 0.65X. The company has wisely lowered investments during the current lower energy commodity price market while pursuing strategic asset swaps with peers (SHEL) to raise output more capital-efficiently.

Although tensions in the Middle East have quieted in recent months, I still see CNQ as a hidden hedge against this issue. In my view, until there is true peace in that region, investors should be aware of the risk that oil prices skyrocket if a conflict impairs Iranian/Saudi energy exports, as well as that of their allies. If this occurs, and a large portion of global energy exports are threatened, oil could rise astronomically, giving immense benefits to North American producers to fill the gap. My view is speculative, and this may hopefully never occur, but I think CNQ is a good way to hedge against this significant portfolio risk.

The Bottom Line

I believe CNQ's negative trend regarding tariff concerns may be a "sell the rumor, buy the news" event. In other words, I think the profit risk of tariffs is generally overstated, given how low the Canadian dollar is in response to that country's economic woes. If 25% tariffs go into effect, I expect the CAD will crash by more than 10%, keeping Canadian oil and gas as competitive as they are today.

However, I will change my view if the US dollar declines, likely requiring a fall in the US economic outlook, which is currently stable. Last week's market declines were shared by stocks and oil, implying that the US economy may be slowing. This could harm domestic oil and gas demand, lowering energy companies' profits.

Accordingly, CNQ faces notable risks and uncertainties today that could cause the stock to fall further. Although I am slightly bullish on it, due mainly to its lower valuation, higher dividend, and my positive views on its management team, I would not be surprised to see CNQ continue to decline over the coming weeks and months. Still, to me, declines in CNQ are a buying opportunity for long-term investors. Further, although I generally expect tariffs to go into effect, I do not currently expect them to last for energy, given they're primarily a "lose-lose" situation for both the US and Canadian economies. If Trump decides to forgo energy tariffs, that could undo CNQ's recent declines.

data.title: ADT Inc.: Earnings Growth Will Drive Share Price Upside
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about ADT Inc. (ADT) previously with a buy rating as the path forward for growth to accelerate was positive in my view. 4Q24 earnings showed the strength of fundamentals, as seen from ADT’s key operating metrics. ADT is also executing well on its ADT+ platform and insurance integrations. I expect ADT to deliver management’s guided FY25 EPS growth and see earnings growth accelerate to low-teens percentage as consensus has expected.

4Q24 earnings showed the fundamental strength of ADT

ADT did well for 4Q24. Total revenue grew to $1.26 billion from $1.172 billion last year. While adj EBITDA margin was lower by ~100bps (down to 52%), absolute adj EBITDA was up from $627 million to $653 million. For FY25, management guided for revenue in the range of $5.025 billion to $5.225 billion, adj EBITDA in the range of $2.65 to $2.75 billion, and adj EPS of $0.77 to $0.85.

I believe the focus for 4Q24 results is beyond the headline figures. ADT’s key operating metrics really showcased how strong ADT’s business is. ADT remains as a business with highly predictable subscription revenue. There are four highlights to note:

  1. The 2% y/y increase in recurring monthly revenue [RMR] to $359 million;
  2. Revenue payback period remained steady at ~2.2 years, a sign that its customer acquisition strategy remains efficient (i.e. it has not reached a stage where marginal returns start to decay strongly);
  3. ADT’s churn reduction efforts are gaining traction – last twelve months [LTM] gross customer revenue attrition came down 20bps y/y to 12.7% y/y. This tells me that ADT strategic shift toward professionally installed systems, combined with better virtual service capabilities, is improving retention.
  4. Lastly, ADT also showed that it has strong pricing power, evident from higher average price realization across monitoring services and professional installations.

ADT+ Adoption Is Solid

ADT’s transition to its proprietary ADT+ platform is also starting to show tangible benefits. Take, for instance, in 4Q24, installation revenue surged 48% y/y to $175 million, clearly reflecting a greater mix of professionally installed systems. Feedback has been positive, and this is an important datapoint to note. As per the transcript, since its national rollout, the platform has maintained an average 4.8-star rating on the Apple App Store and Google Play. I also like that ADT is innovating new features that have resonated with customers. For instance, features like Trusted Neighbor allows users to grant secure access to their homes remotely.

In my opinion, this not only helps drive growth (professionally installed systems have higher revenue per user), it also enhances ADT’s revenue structure because customers purchase equipment upfront. Historically, ADT’s revenue was heavily dependent on acquiring new customers to sustain growth, as the bulk of its business came from recurring monitoring fees. This meant that aggressive customer acquisition efforts were necessary to offset churn and drive revenue growth. However, with ADT+, customers are required to buy Google Nest-integrated equipment upfront as part of a professional installation package, which means a larger portion of ADT’s revenue coming from one-time installation fees rather than purely from ongoing subscriptions. This shift is particularly important because it reduces ADT’s reliance on constantly acquiring new customers to drive revenue growth. You can think of this as a new revenue stream that provides cushion for revenue growth volatility.

State Farm Partnership Channel Momentum continues

ADT’s strategic alliance with State Farm also continues to gain momentum, showing its potential to become a meaningful growth driver. Sales through the State Farm channel reached 18,000 in 2024, up from 5,500 in 2023, reflecting strong demand from homeowners looking to bundle security solutions with their insurance policies. The partnership has now expanded to 17 states, covering ~45% of State Farm’s home policies, with additional pilots underway. I reiterate that the long-term potential here is significant. State Farm’s scale gives ADT a direct pathway to reach millions of customers who might not otherwise consider professional security solutions. Moreover, the introduction of water-leak detection features in select states should enable ADT to cross-sell more products. My view is that so long as ADT can continue to deepen its insurance ecosystem integrations, it will become a strong long-term catalyst for revenue growth.

AI and Virtual Service Supports Margin Expansion

Given that every other company is talking about AI, I should also point out that ADT is benefiting from this AI trend. More specifically, ADT’s investments in AI-driven automation are beginning to pay off, both in terms of cost reduction and customer experience. In 4Q24, AI-powered virtual agents handled 50% of customer service chats, and ADT is targeting this to increase to 90% by 1Q25. Additionally, management is targeting 20% of all voice calls to be handled by AI by year-end. This is a huge step up in automation, and I would imagine this to directly translate into cost savings. My logic is that by automating customer interactions, ADT reduces its reliance on human agents, which means lower labor costs, while maintaining service quality. ADT AI efforts extend beyond just customer service agents, it has also showcased its ability to leverage AI to improve its remote diagnostic capabilities. For those that have missed this data point, ADT now resolves 50% of service issues virtually. You can totally imagine how much cost ADT has saved from reduced deployment of technicians to on-site visits.

Valuation

Management's FY25 guidance calls for 8% EPS growth, which I think sets the baseline to think about ADT future EPS growth. Remember that the ADT+ platform adoption is still at the early phase of ramping upwards and there is still plenty of room for ADT to penetrate in the State Farm partnership. Also, margins should see a structural step up as ADT continues its automation efforts. Consensus FY26 estimate is for adj EPS to reach $0.93 (from adj EPS of $0.75 in FY24), implying ~12%/year adj EPS growth. I think this is a fair estimate given my thoughts on the ADT business as discussed above, and if ADT delivers the expected earnings growth, the stock should be worth ~$9.3 (~15% upside from current share price) assuming the current 10x forward PE valuation. The upside case is that the market attaches a higher multiple because ADT growth and margin profile are structurally better than in the past. If we assume that ADT trades back to its past 3 years average – 12.5x – the upside is even more attractive.

Risk

Fewer home relocations due to rates staying higher for longer could limit new sign-ups. Tariff-related cost increases on equipment sourced from Mexico could also negatively impact margin as subscriber acquisition costs go up. Additionally, no bulk account purchases were made in 4Q24, and none are planned for 2025. This could cause FY25 growth to seem weak, potentially impacting the stock’s sentiment.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for ADT, as I believe it remains well positioned for continued growth, supported by ADT+ adoption and its insurance partnerships. ADT’s AI-driven initiatives should also help it to improve margins structurally. With shares trading at ~10x forward earnings, I see a clear path to 15% upside or more.

data.title: QQQX: 8%-Plus Yield, 9% Discount, Long-Term NAV Growth
data.description:
In Friday's article, we profiled the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), a closed end fund, "CEF", which sells covered call options on NASDAQ stocks, in order to create income for its investors.

This article covers one of JEPQ's competitors, the Nuveen NASDAQ 100 Dynamic Overwrite Fund (NASDAQ:QQQX), another CEF which writes covered calls on the NASDAQ 100.

Fund Profile:

QQQX "is designed to offer regular distributions through a strategy that seeks attractive total return with less volatility than the Nasdaq 100 Index by investing in a U.S. equity portfolio that seeks to substantially replicate the price movements of the Nasdaq 100 Index, as well as selling call options on 35%-75% of the notional value of the Fund's equity portfolio (with a 55% long-term target) in an effort to enhance the Fund's risk-adjusted returns. The strategy will consider the Fund's tax position and employ techniques to improve after-tax shareholder outcomes." (QQQX site)

QQQX has $1.38B in assets, with 284 holdings, an average daily volume of 128K, and an expense ratio of 0.92%. As of 1/31/25, 66% of its holdings were overwritten with call options. It began in January 2007.

Dividends:

QQQX paid a higher quarterly distribution in December '24 of $.56, up from $.42, its steady quarterly dividend since Q1 '23. Based upon its 2/28/25 closing price of $25.91, and its latest distribution of $.56, QQQX's forward yield is 8.65%, whereas its trailing 12-month yield is 7.10%.

QQQX's overall distribution schedule is March/June/Sept./Dec. It generally goes ex-dividend in the middle of the month, and pays at the end of the month, or in the 1st week of the following month.

QQQX's most recent financial report, (Q1-2 '24), showed negative NII of $202K, with $16.15M in net realized gains, vs. $41M in dividends paid. Unrealized appreciation was $163M, up 7% on an annualized basis vs. 2023's total of $284M.

NII and net realized gains both moved at a slower pace in Q1-2 '24, than in full year 2023, when NII was -$113K, and net realized gains were $43.5M.

Taxes:

2024 distributions were estimated to have been comprised of 39.6% in Long-Term gains, 60.4% in Return of Capital, "ROC". ROC offers investors a tax deferral advantage, but it does decrease your tax basis, which will impact your capital gains if you sell your shares.

Holdings:

3 out of QQQX's top 5 industries are tech-related, Semiconductors, Software, and Tech Hardware, Storage & Peripherals, combining for 49.5% of its portfolio, a bit underweight vs. the NASDAQ 100's 58.69% weighting.

It also holds 13.6% in Interactive Media & Services, via its holdings of Meta, and Alphabet. Broadline Retail accounted for 6.2% of its holdings, with 5.9% in Amazon, as of 1/31/25.

Like JEPQ, QQQX's top 10 positions include the so-called Magnificent Seven stocks - Apple, Amazon, Alphabet, Meta, Nvidia, Microsoft, and Tesla, in addition to Cisco and Broadcom. The top 10 comprises ~57% of QQQX's portfolio.

Performance:

QQQX's price performance since inception averaged 10.58%, vs. NAV performance of 11.09%, as of 1/31/25. In general, its price performance has lagged its NAV performance, indicating discounted prices to NAV over the long run.

Its 3-year average shows the biggest discount, with an 8.06% price performance, vs. a 10.26% NAV performance.

QQQX outperformed JEPQ, but lagged the NASDAQ 100 in price during 2024, due to its covered call strategy, which caps upside price gain participation. Its better price performance, and 7.87% yield put its 2024 total return closer of 24.87% closer to QQQ's 25.54% return, vs. 23.48% for JEPQ.

2023 was the opposite, with newish fund JEPQ outperforming QQQX in price by over 800 basis points, and QQQX's total 2023 return of 21.39% lagging JEPQ's 33.65% 2023 return by over 1200 basis points. Both funds lagged the NASDAQ 100 by a wide margin in 2023.

Risks:

Price Risk: CEF's often trade at a discount, which exposes investors to loss of capital. As you'll see in the valuations section, one way to try to avoid that risk is to attempt to buy CEF's at deeper than historical average discounts.

Call Option Risk: Selling calls generates income, but your upside price participation is limited by the strike price of the sold call option. The flip side of that is that the sold call option premiums offer you a buffer in a down market.

Common Stock Price Risk: The underlying equities' prices may fluctuate quickly - for example, look at Tesla's 27% drop so far in 2025. JEPQ is down 4.2% so far in 2025, and most of its top 10 holdings have also declined in price, with the exceptions of Meta and CSCO:

Valuations:

Buying CEF's at a deeper discount than their historical average discounts/premiums can be a useful strategy, due to mean reversion. Since NAV/Share is calculated at the end of each trading day, you have to look at the most recent closing values to determine the current NAV discount or premium.

At its 2/27/25 closing price of $25.73, QQQX was selling at a 9.11% discount to its NAV/share of $28.11. While that's not as deep as its 1-year average discount of 9.85%, it's a lot deeper than its 3-year average discount of 2.66%, and its 5-year average discount of 1.82%.

Parting Thoughts:

We rate QQQX as a speculative Buy, it offers an attractive 8%-plus yield, and has a good long-term track record. Unlike many CEF's, QQQX has increased its NAV/share since inception, growing it by 35%, from $19.10 to $25.73.

All tables furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: The Brink's Company: Path To High Single Digits Growth Remains Intact
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on The Brink’s Company (BCO) was a buy rating because I remained confident that BCO would see growth accelerate to high-single-digit percentages, backed by solid adoption trends seen in the Digital Retail Solutions [DRS] and ATM Managed Services [AMS] segments. I reiterate my buy rating, as I do not see any structural changes to BCO’s ability to accelerate growth.

4Q24 results update

BCO 4Q24 results suggested a mixed headline growth performance, but I believe it showed strong underlying momentum. In 4Q, BCO reported revenue of $1.26 billion, increasing 1.5% y/y. While reported growth seemed weak, organic revenue growth, however, was much stronger at 10.7% y/y, supported by continued momentum in AMS and DRS, which grew 23% y/y. Regionally, every region saw positive growth: North America saw 3% organic revenue growth; Latin America saw 29.4%; Europe saw 5.8% y/y, and the rest of the world saw 1.5% y/y. From a business mix perspective, AMS and DRS now contribute 24% of total revenue on a trailing 12-month [TTM] basis. For profitability, adj EBITDA margin sustained at the ~20% range, coming in at $251 million, and EPS saw $2.12.

AMS and DRS Are Growth Engines

In my view, the key focus for BCO results should be the key growth drivers: AMS and DRS. These segments continue to grow much faster than the rest of the business, growing 23% y/y in 4Q24 and contributing ~$200 million in incremental revenue for the full year. AMS, in particular, has seen a 20% increase in installed devices in FY24, while DRS is benefiting from accelerated rollouts in multiple international markets. If you do the math, AMS and DRS are 24% of total revenue and are growing >20%, which equates to ~5% growth on a consolidated basis.

These segments are not seeing growth slowdown, and I don’t expect growth to slow down given the runway for adoption is still long. The demand for outsourced ATM and retail cash management services remains significant, especially outside of North America. The main underlying driver is that financial institutions [FI], in my opinion, will continue to find ways to reduce costs and improve operational efficiency (these moves by HSBC, Standard Chartered, and Citigroup are clear signs that FI are in cost-optimization mode). One of the most labor-intensive aspects of the banking business is managing cash (this dated article also discussed what I mentioned), and hence, I remained confident that BCO can capitalize on this trend.

As such, I am confident that BCO can achieve the management target that AMS and DRS can maintain double-digit growth, and this should help drive BCO growth back to the high-single-digit range. Additionally, given these are higher-margin businesses, I also see this as a key driver to EBITDA margin expansion over the next few years.

CVM Remains a Cash Generator, Brink’s Global Services Shows Signs of Rebound

Even in BCO’s legacy business—Cash & Valuables Management [CVM]—growth was a lot better than I expected, growing 7% y/y in 4Q24, supported by both pricing and volume growth. What’s interesting here is the traction seen in Brink’s Global Services [BGS]. After a tough few quarters, BGS returned to positive y/y growth in 4Q24, and I expect this momentum to continue into early 2025, driven by higher movements of precious metals like gold and silver into the US. Given the uncertainty in how the USD will react to Trump’s tariff policies, I think this positive demand momentum should continue for the near term due to currency hedging strategies.

Operational Efficiencies and Margin Expansion in Focus

Moving down BCO’s P&L, I believe focus should also be given to how well BCO is making steady progress in improving profitability. While 4Q24 adj EBITDA margin was modestly lower than 4Q23, on an FY24 basis, adj EBITDA margin expanded 40 bps y/y to 18.2%, and I do expect more expansion in the coming years. Apart from higher margin contribution from AMS and DRS, Brink’s Business System [BBS] —BCO’s operational framework—is also a driver. BCO transition toward a more standardized operating model should lead to lower service costs, which means better unit economics. As BCO steps up automation efforts in money processing and benefits from the shift toward a greater mix of AMS/DRS revenue, EBITDA margins should continue to expand in FY25, bringing BCO closer to its 20% medium-term target.

Valuation

I believe the upside remains very attractive. The market does not seem to give credit to the higher growth segments and margin expansion runway. I reiterate my growth assumption that BCO should see growth accelerate to high single-digit percentages in FY27. However, as for margin expansion, given how FY24 has performed, I now see a path for BCO to achieve higher than FY23 margins if margins can continue to expand at ~25bps/year (my previous assumption, which is not a high hurdle given that management guided for FY25 adj EBITDA margin to expand by at least 30bps). If BCO fundamentals perform as I expected, even if multiples do not re-rate higher (stay at the current 12.5% forward P/E), the upside is attractive enough to warrant a buy rating.

Risk

I think the key risk for BCO is still FX fluctuations, in particular, currency devaluations in Argentina, Mexico, and Brazil. Additionally, further regulatory scrutiny could result in more one-off charges (BCO recently settled with the Department of Justice and Financial Crimes Enforcement Network). Lastly, the gradual decline in cash usage, particularly in developed markets, remains a long-term structural risk.

Conclusion

My view for BCO remains a buy rating as the fundamental growth story remains intact. AMS and DRS continue to grow robustly, and I do not see structural headwinds that would impact these segments growth. The long-term trend of FI cutting costs and optimizing cash management continues to benefit BCO. At the current valuation, so long as BCO can grow as I expected, the upside is very attractive, in my opinion.

data.title: Radiant Logistics: Fair Value With M&A Opportunities To Boost Long-Term Growth
data.description:
In November 2024, I marked Radiant Logistics (NYSE:RLGT) a high-risk buy opportunity. The reason to mark the risk was the fact that 2025 earnings were already priced in. Furthermore, the margins in the business are thin. That is not only the case for Radiant Logistics but also for its peers.

Since my buy rating, the stock price has decreased nearly 10%. The stock is still up 8.5% since my first buy rating and is also still showing market outperforming returns. However, in a renewed risk environment, one can wonder how long that outperformance will hold.

In this report, I will update the risk assessment, discuss the most recent earnings and update my price target and rating.

Trade War Casts Shadow On Freight And Logistics

As I pointed out in my risk assessment in August, the risk for Radiant Logistics is any softness in the macroeconomic environment. The company is able to take out some variable costs when market conditions worsen, but even in good times the margins are thin.

Currently, we are seeing that risk is inflating. This is due to a global trade war between the US and its biggest trading partners. President Trump has shared that tariffs against Mexico and Canada will be instated on the 4th of March. Furthermore, additional tariffs against Chinese goods are on the table.

The problem I see with tariffs is that they eventually hurt the consumer and consumer spending might be dented. Consequently, this means that demand for products with or without tariffs declines and freight and logistics service providers have to scale accordingly.

For a business that is already not extremely profitable from a margin perspective, that is a major risk. During the second quarter, 10.5% of the sales of Radiant Logistics were generated from the Canadian activities. In the case of tariffs being implemented, it is not just those revenues that are at risk. The way things seem to be heading now, the global network could be plagued by the consequences of a trade war. Trade wars are rarely isolated events and have widespread consequences.

The opportunities for Radiant Logistics are obviously a stabilization in the demand-supply environment, but also include M&A activity to increase market share and as much as possible to amortize costs. That has worked well for Radiant Logistics as it grew with EBITDA growth outpacing sales growth. Furthermore, it can convert some agent stations into company-owned stations, which could be beneficial to margins.

Radiant Logistics Profits Increase

Second quarter sales increased 31.5% to $264.5 million. The increase was driven by growth in project charters and to a lesser extent through acquisitions. Profits increased from $1.66 million to $8.7 million

What we see in the results is the cost coupling with higher revenues. Cost of transportation service grew more or less in line with revenues in absolute sense. The profit increase was primarily driven by lower operating partner commissions. This demonstrates the margin additions Radiant can achieve through conversions of agent stations.

Margins increased by 0.8% to 3.2%. The margin jump was significant. However, it was almost fully realized by the commission savings. The variable cost component on transportation grew from 69% of sales to 76% of sales. To me, this shows high costs of transportation and the significant cost inflation that occurs when revenues increase.

Radiant Logistics Stock Is Fairly Valued

To determine multi-year price targets The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Wall Street analysts have a $9.75 price target for Radiant Logistics with a strong buy rating. I do not share that optimism. In my view, the stock is currently fairly valued based on the company’s median EV/EBITDA.

Compared to my prior assessment, I have revised the price target down from $9.41 to $8.46. This is driven by the assumption that the company will continue to pursue M&A opportunities. In case there will be macroeconomic pressure, those acquisitions may not be flat growth on top of the current estimates.

However, I am maintaining my buy rating for the simple reason that the company has a good track record on M&A integration.

Conclusion: Radiant Logistics Sees Elevated Risk

I believe that Radiant Logistics is currently experiencing elevated risks driven by the uncertainty of trade. We will have to see how things will develop over time, but the prospect of widespread tariffs is not a great outlook for any company involved in freight and logistics.

Radiant Logistics, however, could benefit in the sense that the elevated risk may reduce its acquisition costs and in fact boost M&A opportunities. These opportunities may not necessarily layer in positively from the start, but strengthen the business for the longer term. As a result, I am maintaining my buy rating.
